BIZ-OMICS · AQA A-Level Economics · Microeconomics

Perfect Competition, Imperfectly Competitive Markets and Monopoly

The full spectrum of market structures — and how competition, or the lack of it, shapes price, output, and efficiency

1

The Spectrum of Market Structures

Real-world markets do not fall neatly into a handful of fixed boxes — they sit along a spectrum, ranging from perfect competition (the most competitive extreme) at one end, to pure monopoly (the least competitive extreme) at the other, with monopolistic competition and oligopoly occupying the middle ground.

Perfect Competition

Many small firms, identical products, free entry/exit, perfect knowledge. Firms are price takers.

Monopolistic Competition

Many firms, differentiated products, relatively free entry/exit. Firms have limited price-making power.

Oligopoly

A few large, interdependent firms, significant barriers to entry. Firms have substantial price-making power.

Monopoly

A single dominant firm, very high barriers to entry, no close substitutes. Firm is a pure price maker.

Fig 1: The spectrum of market structures, from most competitive (left) to least competitive (right).

Moving from left to right along this spectrum, markets generally become less competitive, and individual firms gain progressively greater power to influence the price they charge rather than simply accepting a price set by the wider market.

Why "Spectrum" and Not "Categories": although this chapter examines four distinct named models in turn, AQA expects students to recognise that real-world markets rarely fit any single model perfectly — a market may sit somewhere between monopolistic competition and oligopoly, for example, and its exact position can shift over time as barriers to entry, the number of firms, or the degree of product differentiation change.
2

Distinguishing Market Structures

Three characteristics are used consistently throughout this chapter to place a real-world market at a particular point on the spectrum (Section 1): the number of firms, the degree of product differentiation, and the ease of entry into (and exit from) the market.

Number of Firms
How many firms compete for sales within the market — ranging from a very large number of small firms (perfect competition) to a single firm (monopoly).
Product Differentiation
The extent to which consumers perceive the products of different firms in a market as distinct from one another, whether through genuine differences in quality or features, or through branding and marketing — ranging from completely identical, homogeneous products (perfect competition) to a single, unique product with no close substitutes (monopoly).
Barriers to Entry (and Exit)
Obstacles that make it difficult for new firms to enter a market and compete with existing firms — ranging from completely free entry and exit (perfect competition) to very high barriers that effectively exclude any new competition (monopoly).
CharacteristicPerfect CompetitionMonopolistic CompetitionOligopolyMonopoly
Number of firmsVery many, smallManyFew, largeOne
Product differentiationNone (homogeneous)Some (differentiated)Varies (homogeneous or differentiated)Unique (no close substitutes)
Barriers to entryNoneLowHighVery high / total
Firm's pricing powerNone — price takerLimited — price makerSignificant — price makerSubstantial — price maker
Structure-Conduct-Performance Paradigm
The traditional framework underpinning this entire chapter: the structure of an industry (firm numbers, product differentiation, barriers to entry) shapes the conduct (behaviour) of firms within it — how they set prices, compete, or collude — which in turn determines their performance, measured in terms of efficiency (Sections 30–32) and equity (the level of long-run profit earned).
Why This Paradigm Matters: the Structure-Conduct-Performance paradigm is the underlying logical thread connecting every market structure examined in this chapter — it explains why this chapter analyses structure first (Sections 6, 10, 13, 22), conduct second (pricing and strategic behaviour), and performance last (Sections 30–32). When answering an extended question on any market structure, explicitly tracing this structure → conduct → performance chain is a reliable way to build a well-organised, logically complete answer.
Exam Application: whenever a question asks you to identify or justify a market structure from a description of a real industry, always work through these three characteristics explicitly (number of firms, product differentiation, barriers to entry) rather than asserting a structure without justification — this is consistently one of the most heavily credited skills across this entire topic.
3

Profit Maximisation & the MC=MR Rule

The traditional theory of the firm, used across every market structure examined in this chapter, is built on the assumption that firms aim to maximise profit. This assumption produces a single, powerful decision rule: a firm maximises profit at the level of output where marginal cost equals marginal revenue (MC=MR).

Profit-Maximising Rule (MC=MR)
The proposition that a firm maximises its total profit by producing at the level of output where marginal cost exactly equals marginal revenue.
Fig 2: The profit-maximising rule. A firm produces at output Q₁, where the MC curve crosses the MR curve, and charges the price (P₁) read off the AR (=demand) curve at that output. This single rule applies identically across every market structure in this chapter — only the shape of the AR and MR curves the firm faces changes from one structure to the next.
1
If MC is below MR, producing one more unit adds more to revenue than to cost

The extra unit is profitable, so a rational, profit-maximising firm should produce it.

2
If MC is above MR, producing one more unit adds more to cost than to revenue

The extra unit reduces total profit, so a rational firm should not produce it.

3
Total profit is therefore maximised exactly where MC=MR

At any output below this point, further production is still profitable; at any output beyond this point, further production reduces profit; only at MC=MR is neither true.

Link to the Production, Costs & Revenue Guide: this rule builds directly on the total/marginal relationships established in the companion BIZ-OMICS Production, Costs and Revenue guide — profit is maximised at the output where the gap between total revenue and total cost is greatest, which occurs, by definition, exactly where their rates of change (MR and MC) are equal.
4

The Divorce of Ownership from Control

The simple assumption that "the firm" maximises profit becomes more complicated in large modern companies, where the people who own the firm (shareholders) are typically not the same people who run it day-to-day (professional managers and directors) — a separation known as the divorce of ownership from control.

Divorce of Ownership from Control
The separation, common in large public companies, between the shareholders who own the firm and the professional managers who control its day-to-day decision-making — a separation that can lead managers to pursue objectives other than pure profit maximisation.
Principal-Agent Problem
The conflict of interest that can arise when the agent (managers, acting on the firm's behalf) has different objectives from the principal (shareholders, who own the firm) and possesses more information about the firm's actual operations than the principal does.
Reason for the DivorceConsequence for Firm Behaviour
Managers have greater day-to-day information than shareholdersManagers can pursue their own objectives (Section 5) without shareholders easily detecting or preventing this, since shareholders cannot monitor every decision.
Managers' pay may be linked to firm size or sales rather than profitManagers may pursue growth or higher sales revenue even where this does not maximise shareholder profit, since their own remuneration or status benefits from a larger organisation.
Shareholders are often widely dispersed and have limited individual influenceNo single shareholder may have a strong enough incentive or sufficient voting power to actively monitor and challenge management decisions, weakening the pressure on managers to maximise profit.
★ UK EXAMPLE: RBS and the 2008 Banking Crisis
In the years before the 2008 financial crisis, executive bonus structures at several major UK banks, including Royal Bank of Scotland (RBS), rewarded managers heavily for short-run growth in lending and trading profit, with comparatively little weight placed on the underlying long-run risk being taken on. Managers (agents) had a strong personal financial incentive to pursue rapid, high-risk expansion, while shareholders and, ultimately, UK taxpayers (principals, in the broadest sense, given RBS's subsequent £45.5 billion government bailout) bore the full cost when that risk-taking led to catastrophic losses — a textbook illustration of the principal-agent problem operating at national economic scale.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Collapse of Carillion (2018)
Carillion, a major UK construction and outsourcing firm, collapsed into liquidation in 2018 after years of aggressive accounting that flattered reported profits, partly linked to executive bonus schemes tied to short-term financial targets. Shareholders, pension-scheme members, employees, and thousands of small subcontractors bore the resulting losses, while it later emerged that senior executives had continued receiving substantial bonus payments even as the underlying financial position deteriorated — widely cited by UK regulators and MPs as a case study in the failure of corporate governance to control the divorce of ownership from control.
★ UK EXAMPLE: "Empire Building" Through Mergers and Acquisitions
Because managerial pay, status, and job security are often more closely linked to the overall size of the firm they run than to its profitability (Section 5), managers of large UK public companies have at times been criticised by investment analysts for pursuing large acquisitions that expand the firm's size and the CEO's personal profile, without clear evidence that the takeover will create additional value for shareholders — a pattern commonly referred to as "empire building," and a direct real-world consequence of the principal-agent problem this section describes.
UK Corporate Governance Response: "Say on Pay": reforms to UK company law now give shareholders in listed companies a periodic binding vote on executive remuneration policy, commonly referred to as "say on pay." This is a direct institutional response to the principal-agent problem described above — an attempt to realign managers' incentives more closely with shareholders' interests by giving the principals a formal, enforceable check on how their agents are rewarded.
Exam Application: the divorce of ownership from control is the standard theoretical justification for why real firms might pursue the alternative objectives explored in Section 5, rather than pure profit maximisation as assumed in Section 3 — always use this concept to explain why a firm's actual conduct might diverge from the simple MC=MR prediction.
5

Alternative Objectives & the Satisficing Principle

Because of the divorce of ownership from control (Section 4), real firms often pursue objectives other than pure profit maximisation. AQA specifically identifies several alternative objectives, alongside a distinct decision-making principle known as satisficing.

Survival
Particularly for new or financially fragile firms, simply remaining in business may take priority over maximising profit, especially during periods of economic uncertainty.
Growth (Sales/Revenue Maximisation)
Managers may prioritise growing the firm's size, sales revenue, or market share, since managerial pay, status, and job security are often more closely linked to firm size than to profit.
Quality Maximisation
A firm may prioritise the quality of its product or service above short-term profit, for example to build long-term brand reputation.
Satisficing Principle
Rather than fully maximising any single objective, managers aim to achieve a satisfactory (or "good enough") level of performance across several objectives simultaneously — for example, an acceptable level of profit sufficient to keep shareholders content, while also pursuing growth or quality goals, rather than pushing profit to its absolute theoretical maximum.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Amazon's Growth-Focused Strategy
For much of its history, Amazon deliberately reinvested revenue into growth, warehouse infrastructure, and market share expansion rather than maximising short-run reported profit, at times reporting very thin or negative profit margins despite very large sales revenue. This reflects a strategic prioritisation of long-run market dominance and sales growth over immediate profit maximisation — precisely the kind of alternative objective this section describes, and one that ultimately still serves shareholders through long-run share price growth even without maximising current profit.
Exam Trap to Avoid: pursuing an alternative objective such as growth does not necessarily mean a firm is acting irrationally or against shareholders' interests — it may reflect a considered long-run strategy, or simply the satisficing principle in practice, where a "good enough" outcome across multiple goals is judged preferable to single-mindedly maximising one.
6

Perfect Competition: Assumptions & Characteristics

Perfect competition is a theoretical market structure sitting at the most competitive end of the spectrum (Section 1), built on a specific, demanding set of assumptions that real-world markets can only ever approximate.

Large Numbers of Producers
So many firms operate in the market that no single firm's output decision can influence the market price — each individual firm is too small, relative to the whole market, to have any market power.
Identical (Homogeneous) Products
Every firm's output is perceived by consumers as completely identical, so consumers have no reason to prefer one firm's product over another's beyond price.
Freedom of Entry and Exit
There are no barriers preventing new firms from entering the market when it is profitable to do so, or existing firms from leaving when it is not.
Perfect Knowledge
All buyers and sellers have complete, costless access to all relevant information, including prices charged by every firm and the true quality of every product.
1
Large numbers + identical products mean no single firm can influence price

If one firm tried to charge above the market price, consumers (with perfect knowledge) would switch immediately to any of the countless identical, cheaper alternatives.

2
This makes every firm a "price taker"

Each firm must simply accept the price determined by the interaction of overall market demand and supply, exactly as covered in the companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide.

3
Freedom of entry and exit ensures this discipline persists over time

If existing firms were somehow earning supernormal profit, freedom of entry (Section 8) would attract new firms until that profit was competed away.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Markets That Come Close to Perfect Competition
While no real market fully satisfies all four assumptions, agricultural markets (e.g. wheat or milk production), commodity markets (e.g. crude oil, copper, gold), and foreign exchange markets come unusually close — very large numbers of buyers and sellers, broadly homogeneous products, and prices determined almost entirely by global supply and demand rather than by any single participant's pricing decision.
How the Internet Has Pushed Some Markets Closer to the Model: digital technology has measurably reduced the gap between several real-world markets and the perfect competition assumptions above. Price comparison websites move markets closer to perfect knowledge by allowing consumers to compare prices across sellers almost costlessly. Online selling improves contestability by letting new firms compete without needing costly physical premises — for example, in book and electronics retailing — lowering barriers to entry and increasing the effective number of sellers a consumer can access.
A Theoretical Benchmark, Not a Literal Description: as the specification stresses, no real-world market perfectly satisfies all four assumptions simultaneously — perfect competition instead functions as a theoretical yardstick against which the efficiency of real-world markets can be judged (Section 9), rather than as an accurate description of any specific industry.
7

Perfect Competition in the Short Run

Because a perfectly competitive firm is a price taker (Section 6), it faces a perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve at the ruling market price — meaning AR and MR are both horizontal and identical to each other and to price, a special case of the AR/MR relationship introduced in the companion BIZ-OMICS Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 20.

Fig 3: A perfectly competitive firm earning supernormal profit in the short run. Because the firm is a price taker, P = AR = MR is a single horizontal line. Applying the MC=MR rule (Section 3), the firm produces at the output where MC crosses this line. Since price (P) exceeds average cost (AC) at that output, the firm earns supernormal profit, shown by the shaded rectangle.

In the short run, a perfectly competitive firm may earn supernormal profit (Fig. 3), earn only normal profit, or make a loss, depending on where the market price happens to sit relative to the firm's cost curves at the time.

Fig 4: The short-run shutdown decision. As price falls, a firm should continue producing as long as price at least covers average variable cost (AVC) — even making a loss overall, since any contribution above AVC helps offset fixed costs that must be paid regardless. Only if price falls below the shutdown price (the minimum point of AVC) should the firm cease production immediately, since it would then be losing money on every unit produced, over and above its unavoidable fixed costs. The break-even price (minimum point of ATC) is the price at which the firm earns exactly normal profit.

Worked Example — Applying the Shutdown Rule (Perishable Agricultural Output)

A tomato grower faces a minimum AVC of £10 per bushel and a minimum ATC (break-even price) of £14 per bushel.

If market price = £18: price > ATC → the firm earns supernormal profit and continues producing.
If market price = £12: AVC < price < ATC → the firm makes a loss overall, but continues producing in the short run, since price still covers AVC and contributes towards fixed costs.
If market price = £8: price < AVC → the firm should shut down immediately, since it cannot even cover its variable costs of production.
Fig 3b: A perfectly competitive firm making a loss (subnormal profit) in the short run. The left-hand panel shows how the market-level interaction of demand and supply determines the ruling price P₁ (companion Price Determination guide, Section 16) — the price every individual firm must then accept as given. The right-hand panel shows an individual firm for whom, at this price, AC lies above P₁ at every output level: even at the loss-minimising output where MC=MR, the firm cannot cover its average cost, and makes a loss (shaded). Provided P₁ still exceeds AVC (Fig. 4), the firm continues producing in the short run rather than shutting down immediately.
Why the Firm's Supply Curve Is Its MC Curve Above Shutdown: this shutdown-price analysis is precisely why, as established in the companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide (Section 13), a perfectly competitive firm's short-run supply curve is exactly its MC curve above the minimum point of AVC — below that point, the firm supplies nothing at all, regardless of price.
8

Perfect Competition in the Long Run

Freedom of entry and exit (Section 6) ensures that supernormal profit or loss in the short run (Section 7) cannot persist into the long run.

1
Supernormal profit attracts new entrants

If existing firms are earning supernormal profit (Fig. 3), the absence of barriers to entry allows new firms to enter the industry, attracted by the prospect of the same profit.

2
New entrants increase market supply, driving down price

As total market output rises, the market supply curve shifts right (companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide, Section 12), lowering the equilibrium market price that every price-taking firm must accept.

3
Entry continues until supernormal profit is competed away entirely

New firms keep entering (and price keeps falling) until price falls to exactly the minimum point of each firm's ATC curve, leaving only normal profit — at which point there is no further incentive to enter, and the market reaches long-run equilibrium.

Fig 5: A perfectly competitive firm in long-run equilibrium. Entry has continued until price falls to exactly the minimum point of the ATC curve, where P = MC = ATC(min). The firm earns only normal profit (included within costs, companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 23) — just enough to keep the entrepreneur in this industry, with no supernormal profit left to attract further entry.
Losses Trigger the Reverse Process: exactly the same logic works in reverse for firms making a loss in the short run — freedom of exit allows loss-making firms to leave the industry, reducing market supply and raising price, until the remaining firms are once again earning only normal profit. This symmetry (entry eliminates supernormal profit; exit eliminates losses) is why perfectly competitive firms earn only normal profit in long-run equilibrium, whichever direction the adjustment started from.
9

Perfect Competition as an Efficiency Yardstick

Given certain assumptions — notably the absence of externalities and the four conditions in Section 6 — perfect competition results in an efficient allocation of resources, which is precisely why it is used throughout economics as a yardstick for judging how efficiently real-world markets perform, in both product and labour markets.

Why Fig. 5 Represents Efficiency: in long-run equilibrium (Fig. 5), price equals marginal cost (allocative efficiency, Section 31) and price equals the minimum point of ATC (productive efficiency, Section 31) simultaneously — a combination this chapter shows, in Section 32, that other market structures generally fail to achieve.

Case For Treating Perfect Competition as an Efficient Benchmark

  • Firms are forced by intense competition to produce at the lowest possible cost, or be undercut by rivals (productive efficiency).
  • Freedom of entry and exit continuously reallocates resources towards their most valued, most profitable uses.
  • Consumers pay a price that reflects the true marginal cost of production, with no firm able to exploit market power to charge above this (allocative efficiency).

Limitations of the Yardstick in the Real World

  • The model assumes no externalities (companion BIZ-OMICS Market Failure guide) — in practice, many markets involve significant external costs or benefits the model ignores.
  • Homogeneous products mean no scope for genuine product innovation or variety, which many consumers value.
  • The very small scale implied by "many small firms" may prevent firms from ever reaching minimum efficient scale (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 17), or from funding significant research and development (dynamic efficiency, Section 30).
Exam Application: a strong evaluative answer does not simply assert that "perfect competition is efficient" — it critically assesses this proposition, recognising both the genuine efficiency properties of the model under its assumptions, and the significant real-world limitations (externalities, lack of innovation incentive, unrealistic assumptions) that mean real markets judged against this yardstick may still be performing reasonably well even where they visibly depart from the theoretical model.
10

Monopolistic Competition: Characteristics

Monopolistic competition sits next to perfect competition on the spectrum (Section 1) — sharing many firms and free entry/exit, but crucially replacing perfect competition's identical products with differentiated products, giving each firm a small degree of genuine price-making power.

Monopolistic Competition
A market structure characterised by many firms, differentiated products, and relatively free entry and exit, giving each individual firm a small amount of market power over its own particular version of the product.
CharacteristicDetail
Many firmsEnough firms that each has only a small share of the total market, and firms do not need to consider rivals' individual reactions (unlike oligopoly, Section 13).
Differentiated productsEach firm's product is a close, but not perfect, substitute for its rivals' products — differentiated through genuine quality differences, branding, location, or customer service.
Relatively free entry and exitBarriers to entry are low, though not literally zero as in perfect competition — a new firm typically needs to establish its own brand identity, but faces no major structural barrier.
Downward-sloping AR curveBecause its product is not identical to rivals', a firm can raise price somewhat without losing all its customers (unlike a perfectly competitive price taker), giving it a genuine, if limited, downward-sloping demand curve.
★ UK REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Monopolistic Competition
Independent coffee shops, hairdressers, restaurants, and high-street clothing retailers are classic examples of UK monopolistically competitive markets. Take coffee shops: there are many of them, entry is relatively easy (a lease and modest equipment), and while all sell broadly similar drinks, each differentiates itself through location, atmosphere, brand, and menu — giving a well-loved independent café some ability to charge a slightly higher price than a rival without losing every single customer, unlike a perfectly competitive price taker.
11

Monopolistic Competition: Short Run & Long Run

Because a monopolistically competitive firm has some price-making power (Section 10), it faces a downward-sloping AR curve, with MR lying below it (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 20) — unlike the horizontal AR=MR faced by a perfectly competitive firm (Section 7).

Fig 6: A monopolistically competitive firm earning supernormal profit in the short run. Applying MC=MR (Section 3) to a downward-sloping AR/MR pair, the firm produces at Q, charges price P read from the AR curve, and earns supernormal profit (shaded) since P exceeds ATC at that output — structurally identical to Fig. 3, except that AR now slopes downward rather than being horizontal.

Just as in perfect competition (Section 7), a monopolistically competitive firm may instead make a loss in the short run, if costs are high relative to demand at every output level.

Fig 7: A monopolistically competitive firm making a loss in the short run. Here, even at the profit-maximising (loss-minimising) output where MC=MR, the AC curve lies above the AR curve at every output level — price cannot cover average cost, so the firm makes a loss, shown by the shaded area.
1
Supernormal profit (Fig. 6) attracts new entrants

Relatively free entry (Section 10) allows new firms to launch their own differentiated version of the product, drawn in by the prospect of profit.

2
New entrants take customers from existing firms, shifting their AR curve left

As the market becomes more crowded with similar (though not identical) alternatives, each existing firm's demand curve shifts inward and may become more elastic, as consumers now have more substitutes to switch towards.

3
Entry continues until only normal profit remains

This process continues until each firm's AR curve has shifted left enough to become tangent to (just touching) its ATC curve — at that point, price exactly equals ATC, supernormal profit has been eliminated, and entry stops.

Fig 8: A monopolistically competitive firm in long-run equilibrium. The AR curve is tangent to the ATC curve at the profit-maximising output (where MC=MR), so P = ATC and the firm earns only normal profit — the monopolistic competition equivalent of Fig. 5, but with a downward-sloping AR curve rather than perfect competition's horizontal one, meaning output here occurs where ATC is falling, not at its exact minimum (a key source of the productive inefficiency examined in Section 32).
The Crucial Diagrammatic Difference from Perfect Competition: in long-run equilibrium, a monopolistically competitive firm's output occurs where AR is tangent to ATC, which is not the minimum point of ATC (compare Fig. 8 with Fig. 5) — because AR slopes downward here, the tangency point sits to the left of ATC's minimum. This is the diagrammatic basis for the claim, examined fully in Section 32, that monopolistic competition is productively (though not necessarily allocatively) less efficient than perfect competition, even in long-run equilibrium.
12

Non-Price Competition

Because product differentiation (Section 10) means firms in monopolistic competition are not selling identical goods, competition in these markets extends well beyond price alone into non-price competition — using dimensions other than price to attract customers.

Non-Price Competition
Methods firms use to compete for customers that do not involve changing the price charged, instead focusing on differentiating the product itself, its marketing, or the way it is sold.
Form of Non-Price CompetitionUK Real-World Example
Branding and advertisingIndependent coffee shops building a distinctive local brand identity and loyal following through social media presence and in-store atmosphere, rather than competing purely on the price of a cup of coffee.
Product quality and varietyHigh-street restaurants differentiating through menu variety, ingredient quality, or a distinctive cuisine, rather than through price alone.
Customer serviceIndependent hairdressers building repeat custom through personalised service and relationship with regular clients.
Location and convenienceA café's choice of a high-footfall location as a competitive advantage independent of the price it charges.
Why Non-Price Competition Follows Logically from the Model: because a monopolistically competitive firm's demand curve slopes downward due to product differentiation (Section 10), successful non-price competition that further differentiates a firm's product typically makes its demand curve less elastic (fewer close substitutes remain, from the customer's point of view) and can shift it rightward — directly increasing the firm's potential for supernormal profit in the short run (Fig. 6), even before any entry has occurred.
13

Oligopoly: Characteristics & Real-World Examples

An oligopoly is a market dominated by a small number of large firms, sitting further along the spectrum (Section 1) towards monopoly. Oligopolistic markets vary considerably from one another in terms of firm numbers, the degree of product differentiation, and entry barriers.

Oligopoly
A market structure dominated by a small number of large firms, each of which is large enough, relative to the total market, that its decisions directly affect and are affected by the decisions of its rivals.
Interdependence
The defining feature of oligopoly: because there are only a few large firms, each firm must explicitly take into account how its rivals are likely to react to any change in its own price, output, or marketing strategy before making a decision.
UK Real-World OligopolyFirmsProduct Differentiation
SupermarketsTesco, Sainsbury's, Asda, Morrisons, Aldi, LidlSignificant — branding, store format, and product range vary considerably between chains.
Mobile network operatorsEE, O2, Vodafone, ThreeModerate — broadly similar core service, differentiated through network coverage, bundled content, and pricing plans.
Petrol retailingBP, Shell, Esso, supermarket forecourtsLow — petrol itself is largely homogeneous, with limited genuine differentiation beyond forecourt convenience and loyalty schemes.
Domestic flightsBritish Airways, easyJet, Ryanair (on shared routes)Significant — full-service versus budget/low-cost models differ substantially in service level and pricing structure.
Why Oligopoly Cannot Be Reduced to One Single Model: because oligopolistic industries differ so much in firm numbers, differentiation, and entry conditions, the specification explicitly does not treat any single model (including the kinked demand curve, Section 18) as a complete description of all oligopoly — the defining, common feature across every example above is simply interdependence between a small number of firms, however differently that interdependence plays out in each specific industry.
14

Concentration Ratios

A concentration ratio is the standard quantitative tool used to measure how dominated a market is by its largest firms, and therefore how oligopolistic it is likely to be.

Concentration Ratio
The combined market share of a specified number of the largest firms in an industry, usually expressed as a percentage of total industry sales, output, or employment.

Worked Example — Calculating a Five-Firm Concentration Ratio

Approximate UK grocery market shares: Tesco 27%, Sainsbury's 15%, Asda 14%, Aldi 9%, Morrisons 9%, with the remaining 26% shared across all other retailers.

5-firm concentration ratio = 27% + 15% + 14% + 9% + 9% = 74%

Key point: a 5-firm concentration ratio of 74% indicates a highly concentrated, oligopolistic market — almost three-quarters of all UK grocery spending flows through just five retailers, with the remaining hundreds of grocery businesses in the UK sharing only the remaining 26% between them.

Concentration Ratio LevelInterpretation
Low (e.g. below 40%)Market is relatively unconcentrated — more consistent with monopolistic competition than oligopoly.
High (e.g. above 60–70%)Market is highly concentrated — strongly indicative of oligopoly, with a small number of dominant firms.
UK IndustryApproximate Concentration RatioDetail
Grocery retail5-firm ratio ≈ 74%Tesco, Sainsbury's, Asda, Aldi and Morrisons together account for roughly three-quarters of UK grocery spending (Worked Example above).
Personal current accounts (banking)4-firm ratio ≈ 70%+Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds Banking Group and NatWest Group have long accounted for the large majority of UK personal current accounts, prompting repeated Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) scrutiny of the sector.
Mobile network operators4-firm ratio ≈ close to 100%EE, O2, Vodafone and Three (together with the mobile virtual network operators that lease capacity from them) account for virtually the entire UK mobile subscription market.
Statutory audit of FTSE 100 companies4-firm ratio ≈ 97%+Deloitte, EY, KPMG and PwC (the "Big Four" accountancy firms) audit almost the entire FTSE 100, a concentration level the CMA has specifically investigated and sought to address through audit market reforms.
Duopoly
An oligopoly dominated by just two firms (a high CR2) — the most extreme, concentrated case of oligopoly short of pure monopoly.
A Caveat: Concentration Ratios Only Measure UK Output: concentration ratios are typically calculated using only UK production data, which can be misleading in industries with significant international trade. UK car production, for example, is highly concentrated among a small number of domestic manufacturers — giving a high concentration ratio — but the UK car market a consumer actually faces is far more competitive in practice, since imported cars from dozens of foreign manufacturers compete directly for UK sales. Always consider whether a stated concentration ratio reflects genuine market power, or is simply an artefact of measuring domestic production while ignoring import competition.
Exam Tip: always state which specific concentration ratio is being used (e.g. a 4-firm or 5-firm ratio, as specified in the question or data) — the number of firms included is not fixed, and different choices can give a different impression of how concentrated a market appears.
15

Structure vs Conduct Definitions of Oligopoly

Oligopoly can be defined in two distinct ways: in terms of a market's underlying structure (its objective characteristics, such as firm numbers and concentration), or in terms of firms' actual conduct (how they behave towards one another).

Structural Definition of Oligopoly
Defines oligopoly by objective, measurable market characteristics — typically a high concentration ratio (Section 14), indicating that a small number of large firms account for most of the market's output.
Conduct (Behavioural) Definition of Oligopoly
Defines oligopoly by how firms actually behave towards one another — specifically, whether firms explicitly recognise their mutual interdependence (Section 13) and adjust their own decisions in anticipation of rivals' reactions, regardless of the precise number of firms or concentration ratio involved.
Why Both Definitions Matter: a market could technically have a high concentration ratio (satisfying the structural definition) while its firms behave as if they were in perfect competition, ignoring each other's likely reactions; conversely, a market with a slightly lower concentration ratio could still show classic oligopolistic conduct if its firms are highly aware of, and responsive to, each other's pricing and output decisions. AQA expects students to recognise that the behavioural (conduct) definition is often the more economically meaningful of the two, since interdependence — not simply firm numbers — is the defining feature that shapes real oligopolistic decision-making (Section 19).
16

Collusive vs Non-Collusive Oligopoly

Oligopolists facing mutual interdependence (Section 13) can respond to that interdependence in two broadly different ways: through collusive oligopoly, where firms coordinate their behaviour, or through non-collusive oligopoly, where firms compete independently while still reacting strategically to rivals.

Collusive Oligopoly
A situation in which oligopolistic firms coordinate their pricing, output, or other decisions, typically to reduce the uncertainty of interdependence and increase their joint profits, sometimes forming a cartel (Section 17).
Non-Collusive Oligopoly
A situation in which oligopolistic firms compete independently of one another, without formal or informal coordination, while still explicitly taking rivals' likely reactions into account when making decisions (as modelled by the kinked demand curve, Section 18).
Cooperation vs Collusion
Cooperation is a broader term describing any situation in which firms work together, which can be entirely legal (for example, jointly funding shared industry research or safety standards); collusion specifically refers to firms working together to restrict competition between themselves — typically over price or output — which is illegal under UK and EU competition law when it damages consumer interests.
Collaborative Oligopoly
A distinct situation, separate from collusion, in which oligopoly firms cooperate on a specific activity that genuinely serves the public interest rather than restricting competition to raise price — for example, jointly funding industry-wide research and development, or jointly training workers to raise the skill base of the whole industry, with the resulting knowledge or skills shared across the cooperating firms.
Why Collaborative Oligopoly Is Not the Same as Collusive Oligopoly: both involve firms working together, but the test is whether the cooperation restricts competition and harms consumers (collusion, Section 17) or instead creates genuine shared value without directly fixing prices or restricting output (collaboration) — joint industry training schemes and shared pre-competitive research are typically legal collaboration, while joint price-fixing or output quotas are illegal collusion, even though both might be loosely described as firms "working together."
Why Collusion May Allow Oligopolists to Act as a Joint Monopolist: if all the firms in an oligopoly successfully collude to restrict total industry output and raise price, the industry as a whole can behave exactly like a single profit-maximising monopolist (Section 22) applying MC=MR (Section 3) to the entire market, splitting the resulting supernormal profit between the colluding firms — precisely why competition authorities treat collusion as seriously as they treat monopoly itself.
17

Cartels, Price Leadership & Price Wars

Collusive and non-collusive oligopoly (Section 16) can take several specific real-world forms, each with distinct implications for price, output, and consumer welfare.

Cartel
A formal (or informal) agreement between firms in an oligopoly to fix prices, restrict output, or divide up markets between themselves, in order to increase their joint profits — a specific and typically illegal form of collusion.
Price Leadership
A pattern of behaviour in which one dominant firm (the price leader) sets its price, and other firms in the industry follow by setting similar prices, without any explicit collusive agreement — a form of tacit coordination that can achieve similar outcomes to a formal cartel while being harder to prove or prosecute as illegal collusion.
Price War
A period of aggressive, successive price-cutting between rival oligopolists, each trying to undercut the other to gain market share, typically triggered by the breakdown of collusion or price leadership.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: OPEC as a Cartel
The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) operates as an international cartel, with member countries agreeing collective oil production quotas intended to restrict global supply and support higher prices, mirroring exactly the collusive oligopoly behaviour described above. However, OPEC also illustrates the inherent instability of cartels: individual members frequently have an incentive to secretly produce above their agreed quota (cheating) to capture extra profit for themselves, since the resulting small increase in global supply barely affects the world price — but if enough members cheat simultaneously, the cartel's agreed price can collapse entirely, exactly the cheating incentive formalised in Section 20's game theory analysis.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Supermarket Petrol Price Wars
UK supermarkets have periodically engaged in petrol price wars, with major forecourt operators repeatedly undercutting each other's pump prices to attract price-sensitive motorists into their stores. While beneficial for consumers in the short run through lower prices, sustained price wars can squeeze industry profit margins so severely that they eventually give way to a return to tacit price leadership once the aggressive round of cutting ends.
★ UK EXAMPLE: British Airways & Virgin Atlantic Fuel Surcharge Collusion
In 2007, the UK's Office of Fair Trading fined British Airways approximately £270 million after BA admitted colluding with rival Virgin Atlantic to fix the level of fuel surcharges added to passenger and cargo fares. Over several occasions between 2004 and 2006, BA and Virgin held illegal discussions about surcharge levels rather than competing independently, during which surcharges rose from £5 to £60 per ticket. Virgin Atlantic received full immunity from prosecution as the whistleblower that reported the collusion to regulators — a leniency scheme competition authorities commonly use specifically because cartels are otherwise very difficult to detect from the outside. This case illustrates both the reality of collusive oligopoly in a genuine UK duopoly-style route market, and the seriousness with which UK and EU competition law treats it.
Entry-Limit Pricing
A pricing strategy in which colluding firms (or a dominant firm) deliberately set price below the level that would maximise short-run joint profit, specifically to keep supernormal profit low enough that it does not attract new entrants — sacrificing some short-run profit to protect long-run market dominance.
Why Cartels Might Not Always Set the Simple Joint-Monopoly Price: a cartel does not necessarily set price at the full joint profit-maximising level implied by Section 16 — if barriers to entry are not completely secure, members may collectively choose entry-limit pricing instead, accepting a lower price and lower profit than the theoretical maximum in exchange for a reduced risk of attracting new entrants who would erode their market share and profit in the long run.
18

The Kinked Demand Curve Model

The kinked demand curve model is a classic illustration of non-collusive oligopoly, showing how mutual interdependence and uncertainty about rivals' reactions can lead to relatively stable prices in an oligopolistic market, even when costs change.

Kinked Demand Curve
A demand curve model for a non-collusive oligopolist, in which the firm assumes rivals will follow a price cut (making demand relatively inelastic below the current price) but will not follow a price rise (making demand relatively elastic above the current price), producing a curve with a distinct "kink" at the current price.
Fig 9: The kinked demand curve. Above the kink (price PE, output Q2), AR is relatively elastic — a price rise to P1 loses substantial sales to rivals who don't follow. Below the kink, AR is relatively inelastic — a price cut to P2 gains little extra output (Q3), since rivals match the cut. This produces a discontinuous "gap" in the MR curve directly beneath the kink, shown by the dashed section.
Fig 9b: The kinked demand curve, showing price rigidity directly. The kink in demand at P* produces a vertical, discontinuous "gap" in the MR curve. Because MC1 and MC2 both cross MR somewhere within that gap, a moderate rise in costs (MC1→MC2) still leaves the profit-maximising price and output unchanged at P* and q* — the firm has no incentive to change price even though its costs have genuinely risen, since neither raising price (losing customers to rivals who don't follow) nor cutting price (rivals matching the cut, so little is gained) would improve its position. This is the precise mechanism behind oligopolistic price rigidity.
1
Raising price above P₁ is unattractive

Because demand is elastic above the kink (rivals do not follow a price rise), the firm loses substantial sales and market share to rivals who keep their price unchanged.

2
Cutting price below P₁ is also unattractive

Because demand is inelastic below the kink (rivals immediately follow any price cut to protect their own market share), the firm gains very little extra sales, while total revenue and profit margin both fall.

3
Price tends to stay rigid at P₁, even if costs change moderately

Because the MR curve has a vertical gap directly below the kink (Figs. 9 and 9b), moderate shifts in the MC curve within that gap still intersect MR at the same output level, so the profit-maximising price and output do not change — explaining the price rigidity often observed in real oligopolistic markets.

Use as an Illustration, Not the Only Model: AQA is explicit that the kinked demand curve should be used only as an illustration of interdependence and price rigidity in non-collusive oligopoly, not taught as if it were the single, complete explanation of all oligopolistic behaviour — real oligopolies also engage in collusion (Section 16), price leadership and price wars (Section 17), and strategic behaviour better captured by game theory (Section 20).
19

Interdependence & Uncertainty in Oligopoly

Interdependence (Section 13) and the resulting uncertainty about how rivals will respond are the single most important forces shaping firm behaviour throughout oligopoly, influencing price, output, investment, and spending on research and advertising.

Decision AreaHow Interdependence & Uncertainty Influence It
PriceFirms are often reluctant to change price unilaterally (Section 18), fearing an unpredictable reaction from rivals that could leave them worse off than if they had done nothing.
OutputFirms may deliberately hold spare capacity, partly to be able to respond quickly and credibly to a rival's aggressive move, such as a price cut or a new product launch.
InvestmentLarge, risky, hard-to-reverse investment decisions may be delayed or scaled back due to uncertainty about whether rivals will make matching investments that erode any competitive advantage gained.
Research and advertising expenditureFirms often prefer to compete through non-price competition (Section 12) — heavy advertising and R&D spending — specifically because these are less likely to trigger the same immediate, damaging retaliatory response as a direct price cut.
Why This Section Bridges the Kinked Demand Curve and Game Theory: the kinked demand curve (Section 18) captures uncertainty about a rival's reaction to a single type of decision (pricing); game theory (Section 20) provides a far more general and powerful framework for formally analysing strategic decision-making under interdependence and uncertainty across any of the decision areas in the table above.
20

Game Theory: The Prisoner's Dilemma & Nash Equilibrium

Game theory provides a formal framework for analysing decision-making under exactly the kind of strategic interdependence that characterises oligopoly (Section 13) — where the best choice for one firm depends directly on what its rivals choose to do.

Game Theory
The formal study of strategic decision-making, in which the outcome for each participant ("player") depends not only on their own choices but also on the choices made by other players.
Payoff Matrix
A table showing the outcome (payoff) each player receives for every possible combination of strategies chosen by all players in the game.
Dominant Strategy
A strategy that gives a player their best available outcome regardless of what strategy the other player chooses.
Nash Equilibrium
A stable outcome of a game in which no player can improve their own payoff by unilaterally changing their own strategy, given the strategy currently being chosen by every other player — named after the mathematician John Nash.

Worked Example — A Duopoly Pricing Game (The Prisoner's Dilemma Applied to Oligopoly)

Two firms, A and B, are the only producers in a market. Each must simultaneously choose to set a High Price or a Low Price, without knowing the other's choice in advance. The resulting profits (£m) are shown below.

Firm B: High PriceFirm B: Low Price
Firm A: High PriceA = £50m, B = £50mA = £20m, B = £70m
Firm A: Low PriceA = £70m, B = £20mA = £30m, B = £30m
Firm A's reasoning: if B chooses High, A earns £70m by choosing Low (vs £50m by choosing High) — Low is better.
If B chooses Low, A earns £30m by choosing Low (vs £20m by choosing High) — Low is still better.
⇒ Low Price is a dominant strategy for Firm A, whatever Firm B does. By symmetry, the same is true for Firm B.

Key point: both firms rationally choose Low Price, reaching the Nash equilibrium of (£30m, £30m) — highlighted in the table — even though both firms would have earned more (£50m each) had they both chosen High Price. Neither firm can unilaterally switch back to High Price without making themselves worse off (falling from £30m to £20m), which is exactly why (Low, Low) is a stable Nash equilibrium, while (High, High) is not: at (High, High), each firm has an individual incentive to secretly cheat and cut its price.

This Is the Formal Version of Cartel Instability (Section 17): this exact payoff structure is why real-world cartels such as OPEC (Section 17) are inherently unstable — the jointly best outcome (mutual High Price / restricted output) is not a Nash equilibrium, since each individual member always has a private incentive to cheat and undercut the agreement, just as Firm A and Firm B are each individually drawn towards Low Price above. This is the classic "Prisoner's Dilemma" structure: individually rational decisions by each player produce a collectively worse outcome for both than genuine cooperation would have achieved.
21

Advantages & Disadvantages of Oligopoly

Oligopoly presents a genuinely mixed picture for consumer welfare, with real advantages alongside real disadvantages, depending heavily on whether firms in a particular industry behave collusively or competitively (Section 16).

Potential Advantages of Oligopoly

  • Large firms can achieve significant economies of scale (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 13), potentially passing some cost savings on to consumers.
  • Substantial profits can fund significant research and development, supporting dynamic efficiency and innovation (Section 30).
  • Non-price competition (Section 12) can drive genuine improvements in product quality, variety, and service.
  • Where non-collusive, intense rivalry between a small number of large, well-resourced firms can still produce vigorous competition on price and quality.

Potential Disadvantages of Oligopoly

  • High barriers to entry (Section 13) can protect firms from full competitive pressure, allowing sustained supernormal profit at consumers' expense.
  • Collusion and cartels (Sections 16–17) can allow firms to jointly restrict output and raise prices, mimicking a monopoly's negative welfare effects (Section 24).
  • Price rigidity under the kinked demand curve model (Section 18) can prevent prices from falling even when costs fall, harming consumers.
  • Heavy spending on advertising and branding (Section 12) can raise costs and prices without necessarily improving the underlying product.
Exam Application: a well-evaluated answer on oligopoly should explicitly recognise that its welfare effects depend heavily on firm conduct (Section 15) — a highly collusive oligopoly behaves, and harms consumers, much like a monopoly (Section 24), while a genuinely competitive, non-collusive oligopoly can deliver many of the dynamic benefits of competition (Section 27) despite its high market concentration.
22

Monopoly: Diagrammatic Analysis

A pure monopoly sits at the far end of the spectrum (Section 1) from perfect competition: a single firm supplies the entire market, facing the whole market's downward-sloping demand curve as its own AR curve, with no close substitutes and no competitive constraint on its pricing.

Monopoly
A market structure in which a single firm supplies the entire output of an industry, facing no close substitutes and typically protected by very high or total barriers to entry.
Fig 10: The monopoly model. Applying MC=MR (Section 3), the monopolist produces at Qm, where MC crosses MR (point G), then charges Pm read off the Demand curve at that output (point E). Since Pm exceeds ATCm (point F) at this output, the monopolist earns supernormal ("monopoly") profit, shown by the shaded rectangle. Because Pm > MC at Qm — unlike the perfectly competitive outcome, Fig. 5, where P = MC — the monopolist restricts output below the allocatively efficient level, creating the deadweight loss examined fully in Section 32.
No Distinct "Monopoly Supply Curve": unlike a perfectly competitive firm (companion Price Determination guide, Section 13), a monopolist does not have a well-defined supply curve showing a unique quantity for every price — because the monopolist is a price maker choosing a single point on its AR curve via MC=MR, the same MC curve could be associated with different price/output combinations depending on the shape of demand, breaking the simple one-to-one price/quantity relationship a supply curve requires.
23

Sources of Monopoly Power

A firm's degree of monopoly power — how far its pricing behaviour departs from a competitive price taker — is influenced by several interrelated factors.

Source of Monopoly PowerExplanation
Barriers to entryThe higher the barriers (patents, high minimum efficient scale, control of an essential resource, legal restrictions), the more secure a firm's dominant position and the greater its pricing power, since it faces little threat of new competition.
Number of competitorsThe fewer genuine competitors a firm faces, the less constrained its pricing decisions are by the risk of losing customers to rivals.
Advertising and brandingHeavy, sustained advertising can build strong brand loyalty, making consumers less willing to switch even if a rival offers a lower price, effectively reducing the price elasticity of demand the dominant firm faces.
Degree of product differentiationThe more a firm's product is perceived as genuinely unique, with no close substitutes, the greater its ability to raise price without losing significant sales.
Network externalities ("applications barrier to entry")Where the value of using a product rises as more other people also use it, an already-dominant firm benefits from a self-reinforcing advantage — consumers and third-party developers cluster around the existing standard, making it very difficult for a technically equivalent rival product to attract users away, regardless of price or quality.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Monopoly and Near-Monopoly Power in the UK
National Grid holds a legal monopoly over the UK's high-voltage electricity transmission network, since duplicating national transmission infrastructure would be enormously costly and inefficient — a classic natural monopoly protected by extremely high barriers to entry. Royal Mail retains a monopoly over the universal postal service obligation (guaranteed delivery to every UK address at a uniform price), even though parcel delivery itself is now a competitive market. Google holds a very high degree of monopoly power in UK search advertising, sustained less by legal barriers than by strong network effects and brand dominance that make it very difficult for rival search engines to attract sufficient advertisers and users simultaneously.
★ CLASSIC EXAMPLE: Microsoft and the "Applications Barrier to Entry"
Microsoft's historical dominance of desktop operating systems (Windows) and office software (Word, Excel) is a textbook case of network externalities acting as a barrier to entry. Because so many consumers and businesses already used Windows, software developers had a strong incentive to write applications for Windows rather than rival systems, which in turn made Windows more attractive to consumers — a self-reinforcing cycle that made it extremely difficult for a rival operating system to gain a foothold, regardless of its technical merits. It has taken many years, and the emergence of entirely new computing platforms (smartphones, cloud-based services from Google and others), for this dominance to face serious competitive challenge — consistent with the creative destruction process examined in Section 28.
24

Advantages & Disadvantages of Monopoly

As with oligopoly (Section 21), monopoly presents a genuine mix of potential benefits and costs, rather than being unambiguously harmful to consumer welfare in every case.

X-Inefficiency
A further, dynamic source of productive inefficiency, distinct from simply not producing at the minimum point of the ATC curve (Section 31) — the tendency for firms shielded from competitive pressure by high barriers to entry to develop organisational slack, overstaffing, and resistance to change, so that any given level of output ends up costing more to produce than it would under genuine competitive pressure. Diagrammatically, X-inefficiency shifts the entire ATC curve upward, rather than simply failing to reach the minimum of an unchanged ATC curve.
Why X-Inefficiency Is a Serious Additional Criticism: the allocative and productive inefficiency shown in Fig. 10 (Section 22) already assumes the monopolist's ATC curve is drawn correctly and minimised for its technology. X-inefficiency argues that a monopolist insulated from competition has weaker pressure to actually minimise its ATC curve in the first place — without rivals threatening to undercut an inefficient firm, management may tolerate unnecessary costs, since even an inefficient monopolist can typically still earn acceptable profit. This makes X-inefficiency a genuinely additional, dynamic criticism of monopoly, beyond the static deadweight loss already captured in Fig. 10.

Potential Advantages of Monopoly

  • A single large firm may achieve substantial economies of scale (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 13) unavailable to many smaller competing firms, potentially lowering average costs.
  • Guaranteed supernormal profit (Fig. 10) can fund large-scale research and development, supporting dynamic efficiency (Section 30) — the Schumpeterian argument that monopoly profit is a necessary reward that funds future innovation.
  • For natural monopolies (e.g. national grid infrastructure), a single provider avoids the wasteful duplication of infrastructure that competition would otherwise require.
  • A monopolist may be better placed to invest in long-term projects, insulated from short-term competitive pressure.

Potential Disadvantages of Monopoly

  • Higher prices and lower output than under competition (Fig. 10), reducing consumer surplus and creating a deadweight loss of economic welfare (Section 32).
  • Absence of competitive pressure may reduce the incentive to minimise costs or innovate, potentially leading to productive inefficiency (Section 31) and X-inefficiency (above).
  • Very high barriers to entry can persist indefinitely, entrenching monopoly power with no natural competitive check.
  • A monopolist with substantial market power may be able to price discriminate (Sections 25–26), extracting additional consumer surplus.
Exam Application: the strongest evaluative answers on monopoly explicitly weigh the static efficiency losses (higher prices, deadweight loss, Section 32) against the potential dynamic efficiency gains (innovation funded by supernormal profit, Section 30) — this static-versus-dynamic efficiency trade-off is one of the most consistently rewarded lines of evaluation across this entire topic.
25

Price Discrimination: Conditions

Price discrimination occurs when a firm with market power charges different prices to different consumers (or groups of consumers) for an identical product, where those price differences are not explained by differences in the cost of supplying them.

Price Discrimination
The practice of charging different prices to different consumers or groups of consumers for the same good or service, where the price difference does not reflect a difference in the cost of production or supply.
Necessary ConditionExplanation
The firm must possess some degree of monopoly powerWithout market power (Section 23), a firm cannot set its own price above the competitive level for any group of consumers — price discrimination is therefore only possible outside of perfect competition.
Different consumer groups must have different price elasticities of demandThe firm must be able to identify groups whose demand is relatively price inelastic (willing to pay more) and groups whose demand is relatively price elastic (only willing to buy at a lower price), consistent with the elasticity theory in the companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide, Sections 3–6.
The firm must be able to prevent resale (arbitrage) between the groupsIf consumers who bought at the low price could easily resell to consumers who would otherwise pay the high price, the price discrimination strategy would collapse — the firm must be able to separate its markets effectively.
★ UK REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Price Discrimination
UK rail operators charge substantially higher fares for peak-time travel than off-peak travel, and offer discounted Railcards to groups such as students and older people whose demand for rail travel tends to be more price elastic — while making resale of personalised, ID-checked tickets impractical. Cinemas commonly charge lower prices for students, children, and senior citizens than for standard adult tickets, exploiting the same elasticity differences, with resale prevented by requiring ID at the point of entry. Airlines charge substantially more for tickets booked close to the departure date (targeting relatively inelastic, time-pressured business travellers) than for tickets booked well in advance (targeting more price-elastic leisure travellers), with resale prevented by requiring the ticket to match the named passenger.
26

Price Discrimination: Diagram & Impact

Price discrimination allows a firm to capture more of the total available consumer surplus as extra profit than it could by charging a single uniform price to every consumer.

Fig 11: Third-degree price discrimination — the standard diagrammatic analysis. The firm splits total market demand into two sub-markets with different price elasticities of demand (Section 25). In each sub-market, the firm applies MC=MR independently: Sub Market B (more inelastic demand) is charged a higher price with a larger price–MR gap (larger pink rectangle relative to output); Sub Market A (more elastic demand) is charged a lower price. The combined output and revenue from both sub-markets make up the total market shown on the right, where the firm's single MC curve determines overall output.
Fig 12: First-degree (perfect) price discrimination compared with monopoly. Under perfect price discrimination (left), the firm charges every single consumer the maximum price they are individually willing to pay, so the AR curve itself becomes the firm's MR curve — the firm produces all the way out to where demand meets MC, converting the entire area under demand and above MC into producer surplus, with no deadweight loss at all. Under ordinary single-price monopoly (right), by contrast, the firm restricts output to Qm and leaves both a deadweight loss (yellow) and some consumer surplus (teal) unclaimed. This is the diagrammatic basis for the point made in Section 32: price discrimination can, in principle, reduce or even eliminate the deadweight loss associated with monopoly power, even as it redistributes the resulting surplus away from consumers and towards the firm.

Advantages of Price Discrimination

  • Firms capture additional revenue, which can fund investment, research and development, or cross-subsidise loss-making services (e.g. off-peak or rural rail services).
  • Consumers with more elastic demand (often lower-income groups, such as students or those able to travel off-peak) gain access to the product at a lower price than a single uniform price might allow.
  • By serving both segments rather than being forced to pick a single price, total output and consumption may be higher than under uniform pricing, potentially raising overall economic welfare.

Disadvantages of Price Discrimination

  • Consumers with relatively inelastic demand (Section 25) pay a higher price than they would under a single uniform market price, redistributing consumer surplus towards the firm.
  • Requires the firm to have and exploit market power, itself a potential source of the wider disadvantages associated with monopoly (Section 24).
  • Can be perceived by consumers as unfair, particularly where price differences are not clearly linked to any genuine difference in cost or value received.
27

The Benefits of Competition

Having examined the full spectrum from perfect competition to monopoly, this section draws together the short-run and long-run benefits that competition, in general, tends to deliver — benefits that extend well beyond price alone.

Type of BenefitExplanation
Short-run price benefitsCompetitive pressure between rival firms tends to push prices closer to the competitive (allocatively efficient) level, as demonstrated by the contrast between Fig. 5 (perfect competition) and Fig. 10 (monopoly).
Short-run cost disciplineFirms facing genuine competition cannot survive with excessive costs relative to rivals, creating strong pressure towards productive efficiency (Section 31).
Long-run product improvementCompetition drives firms to continuously strive to improve their product's quality, features, and design, rather than allowing quality to stagnate under the protection of monopoly power.
Long-run cost reductionCompetitive pressure provides an ongoing incentive to seek out new, lower-cost production methods, rather than tolerating organisational slack.
Long-run service qualityFirms compete to improve the overall quality of service provided to consumers, including responsiveness, reliability, and customer support, as part of non-price competition (Section 12).
Long-run innovationCompetition provides a continuous incentive for innovation, driving the creative destruction process examined in Section 28.
Competition Is Not Only About Price: as this table demonstrates, the specification is explicit that firms compete along many dimensions simultaneously — a market with relatively stable prices (such as an oligopoly following the kinked demand curve, Section 18) may nonetheless be highly competitive in terms of product quality, service, and innovation, and should not automatically be judged as delivering poor outcomes for consumers on the basis of price rigidity alone.
28

Creative Destruction Revisited

As introduced in the companion BIZ-OMICS Production, Costs and Revenue guide (Section 26), creative destruction describes how innovation continuously creates new products and firms while destroying the old ones they replace. In the context of market structure, this process is the fundamental long-run mechanism by which even highly concentrated markets remain subject to competitive pressure over time.

1
Firms with monopoly power earn large supernormal profits

High barriers to entry (Section 23) allow these profits to persist far longer than they could under perfect competition (Section 8).

2
Large, persistent profits create a strong incentive for entrepreneurs to innovate around existing barriers

Rather than competing head-on within the existing barriers, challengers seek entirely new technologies or business models that make the incumbent's barriers to entry irrelevant.

3
Successful innovation destroys the incumbent's market power, even without ever directly attacking its original barriers

The incumbent's dominant position collapses not because a rival out-competed it within the old market, but because the old market itself has been rendered obsolete.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Creative Destruction Overturning Market Power
Kodak held an extremely dominant position in photographic film for most of the twentieth century, protected by strong brand loyalty and technical expertise — yet the innovation of digital photography, rather than a rival film manufacturer, destroyed this position almost entirely, ultimately leading to Kodak's 2012 bankruptcy filing. Smartphones subsequently destroyed the previously separate markets for dedicated digital cameras, MP3 players, and satellite navigation devices, as a single innovative product absorbed the functions of several previously distinct industries. Streaming and e-commerce platforms have similarly displaced significant parts of traditional UK high-street retail, illustrating that even firms with substantial existing market power (Section 23) remain vulnerable to innovation-driven creative destruction in the long run.
Why This Matters for Competition Policy: the prospect of creative destruction is a key argument for why policymakers may sometimes tolerate short-run monopoly power (Section 24) — if today's dominant firm can reasonably be expected to eventually be displaced by tomorrow's innovator, the case for aggressive intervention against its current market power is weaker than if that dominant position were genuinely permanent and unchallengeable.
29

Contestability, Sunk Costs & Hit-and-Run Competition

The theory of contestable markets argues that the number of firms currently operating in a market may matter less for competitive behaviour than how easily new firms could enter if existing firms started earning high profits — a market's contestability can discipline even a monopolist's behaviour, without a single additional firm ever actually entering.

Contestable Market
A market in which entry and exit are free and costless, particularly with respect to sunk costs, such that existing firms face a constant, credible threat of new entry if they earn supernormal profit.
Sunk Costs
Costs that, once incurred, cannot be recovered if a firm exits the market — for example, highly specialised equipment with no resale value or alternative use outside this specific industry. Low sunk costs are essential for a market to be genuinely contestable.
Hit-and-Run Competition
A pattern of behaviour possible in highly contestable markets, in which a new firm enters a market that is temporarily earning high profit, captures some of that profit, and then exits again quickly and at low cost if incumbents respond by cutting price — deterring the very existence of persistent high profit in the first place.
1
Low sunk costs mean entry carries little financial risk

If capital used to enter a market could easily be redeployed or resold elsewhere with little loss, a new firm risks very little by trying its luck in a currently profitable market.

2
This makes the threat of entry credible, even if no entry has actually occurred yet

Existing firms know that if they set price too high, hit-and-run entrants will be attracted in almost immediately.

3
Existing firms are therefore disciplined into behaving competitively, regardless of how few firms currently operate in the market

A market with only one or two current firms can still deliver close to competitive prices and output, provided it is highly contestable — directly challenging the simple assumption that firm numbers alone determine competitive outcomes (Section 2).

Fig 13: The disciplining effect of contestability. In a non-contestable market (left), a dominant firm applies MC=MR as normal, charging PM and earning supernormal profit (shaded, since PM > CM) at point A, protected from entry by high sunk costs. In a highly contestable market (right), the credible threat of hit-and-run entry forces the same firm to abandon its profit-maximising price and instead set price much closer to average cost at point B (PC), even though the number of firms actually operating in the market may not have changed at all — contestability disciplines pricing behaviour directly, without requiring real entry to occur.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Contestability in Budget Airline Routes
European short-haul airline routes are often cited as relatively contestable, since aircraft (the main capital investment) can be redeployed to a different, more profitable route relatively quickly and at low cost if a particular route proves unprofitable, meaning sunk costs specific to any one route are low. This allows budget airlines such as easyJet and Ryanair to enter and exit specific routes flexibly in response to profitability, exerting a competitive discipline on incumbents even on routes served by very few airlines at any one time — a sharp contrast with the UK rail industry, where dedicated track and station infrastructure represent enormous sunk costs, making the rail market far less contestable.
30

Static vs Dynamic Efficiency

Assessing how well different market structures perform requires distinguishing between static efficiency, measured at a single point in time, and dynamic efficiency, measured over time as an industry evolves.

Static Efficiency
Efficiency measured at a single point in time, encompassing productive efficiency and allocative efficiency (Section 31) — how well an industry is using its current resources and technology, given a fixed level of knowledge and capability.
Dynamic Efficiency
Efficiency in the way an industry improves and innovates over time, driven by factors such as research and development, investment in human and non-human capital, and technological change — whether an industry is getting better at producing over time, not just how well it is producing right now.
Why This Distinction Drives So Much Evaluation in This Topic: perfect competition (Section 9) tends to score well on static efficiency, but its very small firms, thin profit margins, and homogeneous products may leave little scope or funding for significant research and development, potentially limiting dynamic efficiency. Monopoly and oligopoly (Sections 21 and 24) tend to perform worse on static efficiency, but their larger scale and greater supernormal profit may fund more research and development, potentially delivering superior dynamic efficiency over time — the Schumpeterian argument referenced in Section 24. Almost every extended-response question comparing market structures in this topic can be substantially strengthened by explicitly applying this static-versus-dynamic distinction.
31

Productive & Allocative Efficiency

Static efficiency (Section 30) is itself made up of two distinct conditions: productive efficiency and allocative efficiency.

Productive Efficiency
A firm or industry is productively efficient when it produces at the lowest possible average total cost — that is, at the minimum point of the ATC curve.
Allocative Efficiency
A firm or industry is allocatively efficient when price equals marginal cost (P = MC) — meaning resources are allocated exactly according to consumers' willingness to pay for the last unit produced, relative to the true cost of producing it.
Market StructureProductively Efficient?Allocatively Efficient?
Perfect competition (long run, Fig. 5)Yes — P = ATC at ATC's exact minimum point.Yes — P = MC, since MC crosses AR (=MR=P) exactly at ATC's minimum.
Monopolistic competition (long run, Fig. 8)No — the AR/ATC tangency point occurs where ATC is still falling, to the left of its minimum.No — P > MC at the profit-maximising output, since MR (and therefore MC at MC=MR) lies below the downward-sloping AR curve.
Monopoly (Fig. 10)Not necessarily — there is no competitive pressure forcing output to ATC's minimum, though economies of scale may still be substantial (Section 24).No — P > MC at Qm, exactly the gap that generates the deadweight loss triangle in Fig. 10.
Why Only Perfect Competition Achieves Both Simultaneously: because a perfectly competitive firm faces a horizontal AR=MR=P line (Section 7), the MC=MR profit-maximising condition automatically becomes P=MC (allocative efficiency), and long-run entry (Section 8) forces this to occur exactly where ATC is at its minimum (productive efficiency) — the two conditions collapse into a single point only because AR is horizontal. Every other market structure examined in this chapter has a downward-sloping AR curve, which structurally separates these two conditions and prevents both being achieved simultaneously.
32

Efficiency Across Market Structures & Deadweight Loss

Bringing together Sections 30 and 31, the efficiency properties of each market structure can now be directly compared, including the specific measurable cost that market power imposes on society: the deadweight loss of welfare.

Deadweight Loss (Welfare Loss)
The loss of total economic welfare (combined consumer and producer surplus) that occurs when a market fails to reach the allocatively efficient level of output, typically because a firm with market power restricts output below the competitive level to raise price and profit.
Reading the Deadweight Loss Directly Off Fig. 10: compared with a hypothetical competitive outcome at the same industry cost structure (where price would equal MC, at the point where the MC curve crosses the AR/demand curve), the monopolist in Fig. 10 restricts output from that competitive level down to Qm and raises price from the competitive level up to Pm.
