The red-shaded triangle in Fig. 10 represents mutually beneficial trades between consumers and producers that would have occurred at the competitive output level, but no longer take place under monopoly — a permanent loss to total economic welfare with no offsetting gain to anyone, which is precisely why it is called a "deadweight" (pure) loss.
Market StructureStatic Efficiency (Section 30)Likely Dynamic Efficiency (Section 30)
Perfect competitionHigh — both productively and allocatively efficient in long-run equilibrium (Section 31).Potentially limited — thin profit margins may restrict funding for research and development.
Monopolistic competitionModerate — allocatively and productively inefficient, but the inefficiency is typically small given the closeness of competition.Moderate — some scope for product innovation as a form of non-price competition.
OligopolyLow to moderate — depends heavily on the degree of collusion (Section 16); can range from close to competitive outcomes to close to monopoly outcomes.Potentially high — substantial resources available for research, development, and advertising-driven innovation.
MonopolyLow — allocatively inefficient by definition, with a measurable deadweight loss (Fig. 10); productive efficiency not guaranteed without competitive pressure.Potentially high — guaranteed supernormal profit could fund substantial long-run innovation, per the Schumpeterian argument (Section 24).
Applying This to Price Discrimination and Welfare: as introduced in Section 26, price discrimination can, in some circumstances, actually increase total output towards the competitive level (since the firm no longer needs to restrict output to protect a single uniform price), potentially reducing the deadweight loss shown in Fig. 10, even while redistributing consumer surplus away from inelastic-demand consumers and towards the firm's profit — a genuinely ambiguous overall welfare effect that rewards careful, balanced exam evaluation rather than a simple "good" or "bad" verdict.
33

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Market Power to Deadweight Loss

High barriers to entry and limited close substitutes give a firm significant monopoly power (Section 23) → the firm faces a downward-sloping AR curve rather than perfect competition's horizontal one → applying the profit-maximising rule MC=MR (Section 3) to this downward-sloping AR/MR pair produces an output where P > MC (Fig. 10) → because P > MC, the market is allocatively inefficient (Section 31) → the resulting gap between the competitive and actual output levels generates a permanent deadweight loss of economic welfare (Section 32), the core efficiency cost of monopoly power.

The Golden Chain: From Interdependence to Cartel Instability

A small number of large firms in an oligopoly are mutually interdependent (Section 13) → to reduce the resulting uncertainty and raise joint profits, firms have an incentive to collude, potentially forming a cartel that behaves like a joint monopolist (Section 16) → however, each individual firm also has a private incentive to secretly cheat on the agreement, since undercutting rivals while they hold to the high price captures extra profit for the cheating firm alone (Section 20's game theory payoff matrix) → this cheating incentive makes the collusive (High Price, High Price) outcome unstable, since it is not a Nash equilibrium → the cartel tends to break down towards the competitive (Low Price, Low Price) Nash equilibrium, explaining why real-world cartels such as OPEC persistently struggle to enforce their agreements (Section 17).

Key Exam Tips

1

Always begin market structure questions by explicitly identifying firm numbers, product differentiation, and barriers to entry (Section 2) before applying a specific diagram — this justifies your choice of model and is separately credited.

2

For any profit-maximisation diagram, always apply MC=MR first to find output, then read price off the AR curve at that output — never read price directly from the MR curve, a very common error.

3

Remember the key visual difference between short-run and long-run equilibrium in perfect competition and monopolistic competition: in the long run, entry/exit has occurred and AR has shifted until only normal profit remains (Figs. 5 and 8) — do not present a short-run supernormal-profit diagram when a question specifically asks about the long run.

4

When discussing oligopoly, explicitly state whether you are describing collusive or non-collusive behaviour (Section 16) before analysing likely outcomes — the welfare implications of oligopoly differ enormously between the two.

5

For game theory questions, always identify the dominant strategy for each player first (if one exists), then identify the Nash equilibrium as the strategy combination where neither player can unilaterally improve their payoff — and explicitly note if the Nash equilibrium is not the jointly best outcome, the classic Prisoner's Dilemma insight (Section 20).

6

For efficiency evaluation questions, always distinguish productive efficiency (P=minimum ATC) from allocative efficiency (P=MC) explicitly (Section 31), and weigh static efficiency against dynamic efficiency (Section 30) — this combination is the single most rewarded evaluative structure across the entire market structures topic.
